answer:
**Charles Comte dedicates Chapter 4 of *Traité de la propriété: VOL I* (titled "De l'occupation des choses," translated as "On the occupation of things") to this concept.[^1]**

He opens the chapter by defining it explicitly as **"the act of taking possession of a thing that has no master, with the intention of appropriating it"**, noting that jurists have long viewed it as a principal means of acquiring property.[^2]

Comte elaborates throughout the chapter (33 paragraphs), explaining its natural basis and universal recognition prior to legislation. A core explanatory passage states:

> But when a thing has never had a master, and can nevertheless satisfy the needs of a person, to whom must one guarantee its exclusive enjoyment and disposition? **To the first who seizes it, with the intention of appropriating it**; for it is probable that it suits him better than any other person, since before any other, he has seized it. The fact of **occupation** always requires that one devote oneself to certain labors... He who takes possession of a thing that has no master despoils no man of his means of existence... In considering the **occupation of things** not yet appropriated as one of the first means of acquiring property, nations have therefore obeyed a law of their nature.[^3]

This builds on earlier remarks, such as **"the act of occupation of a thing that belongs to no one, suffices to make that thing the property of him who takes possession of it"**.[^4]

The term is not a one-sentence definition but a foundational idea unpacked across the chapter; Chapter 5 shifts to appropriation more generally.[^5]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch.4 ("De l'occupation des choses"), [full chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-ab8596](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-ab8596).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-8094b8](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-8094b8).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-041c9e](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/04-de-loccupation-des-choses/translation/#p-041c9e).

[^5]: Ibid., ch.5 ("De l'appropriation des choses en général"), [full chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/05-de-lappropriation-des-choses-en-general/translation/).
